Adding OEWS wage and employment context

Source: vignettes/oews-wage-context.Rmd
oews-wage-context.Rmd

O*NET describes the tasks, skills, abilities, and work contexts associated with occupations. The Bureau of Labor Statistics Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OEWS) program adds labor-market scale: employment counts and wage estimates by SOC occupation code.

This vignette uses a small bundled OEWS sample so it can run without network access. For real analyses, call onet_oews_national(), onet_oews_state(), onet_oews_metro(), or onet_oews_industry() without path.

library(onet2r)
library(dplyr)
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'dplyr'
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
#> 
#>     filter, lag
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
#> 
#>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

sample_oews <- system.file(
  "extdata",
  "oews-national-sample.csv",
  package = "onet2r"
)

Read OEWS estimates 

oews <- onet_oews_national(year = 2024, path = sample_oews)
oews
#> # A tibble: 3 × 7
#>    year oews_type occ_code occ_title           tot_emp a_median h_median
#>   <int> <chr>     <chr>    <chr>                 <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1  2024 national  15-1252  Software Developers 1847900   133080     64.0
#> 2  2024 national  29-1141  Registered Nurses   3175400    93070     44.8
#> 3  2024 national  11-1011  Chief Executives     211230   206680     99.4

The parser normalizes column names to snake_case and converts OEWS numeric fields such as employment and annual median wages from formatted strings to numeric columns.

str(oews)
#> tibble [3 × 7] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
#>  $ year     : int [1:3] 2024 2024 2024
#>  $ oews_type: chr [1:3] "national" "national" "national"
#>  $ occ_code : chr [1:3] "15-1252" "29-1141" "11-1011"
#>  $ occ_title: chr [1:3] "Software Developers" "Registered Nurses" "Chief Executives"
#>  $ tot_emp  : num [1:3] 1847900 3175400 211230
#>  $ a_median : num [1:3] 133080 93070 206680
#>  $ h_median : num [1:3] 64 44.8 99.4

Join OEWS to O*NET occupation codes

O*NET-SOC codes commonly include a detail suffix, such as .00. OEWS uses six-digit SOC codes. onet_join_oews() creates a soc_code column and joins on that normalized code.

occupations <- tibble::tibble(
  code = c("15-1252.00", "29-1141.00", "11-1011.00"),
  title = c("Software Developers", "Registered Nurses", "Chief Executives")
)

occupation_wages <- onet_join_oews(occupations, oews = oews)
occupation_wages
#> # A tibble: 3 × 9
#>   code       title  soc_code  year oews_type occ_title tot_emp a_median h_median
#>   <chr>      <chr>  <chr>    <int> <chr>     <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1 15-1252.00 Softw… 15-1252   2024 national  Software… 1847900   133080     64.0
#> 2 29-1141.00 Regis… 29-1141   2024 national  Register… 3175400    93070     44.8
#> 3 11-1011.00 Chief… 11-1011   2024 national  Chief Ex…  211230   206680     99.4

Employment-weighted O*NET summaries

Many research workflows need to turn occupation-level O*NET records into labor-market-weighted measures. For example, the same skill can appear in multiple occupations with different skill ratings and very different employment levels.

skill_rows <- tibble::tibble(
  code = c("15-1252.00", "15-1252.00", "29-1141.00", "29-1141.00"),
  element_id = c("2.A.1.a", "2.A.1.b", "2.A.1.a", "2.A.1.b"),
  element_name = c(
    "Reading Comprehension",
    "Active Listening",
    "Reading Comprehension",
    "Active Listening"
  ),
  data_value = c(4.12, 4.00, 3.88, 4.25)
)

onet_weighted_summary(
  skill_rows,
  group = c("element_id", "element_name"),
  value = "data_value",
  oews = oews
)
#> # A tibble: 2 × 7
#>   element_id element_name     n_records n_occupations total_weight weighted_mean
#>   <chr>      <chr>                <int>         <int>        <dbl>         <dbl>
#> 1 2.A.1.a    Reading Compreh…         2             2      5023300          3.97
#> 2 2.A.1.b    Active Listening         2             2      5023300          4.16
#> # ℹ 1 more variable: wage_weighted_mean <dbl>

The output includes:

  • n_records: number of rows used in each grouped summary
  • n_occupations: number of occupations contributing to the summary
  • total_weight: sum of employment weights
  • weighted_mean: employment-weighted mean of the selected O*NET value
  • wage_weighted_mean: mean weighted by employment multiplied by annual median wage

PUMS employment weights

If your workflow uses ACS or CPS microdata instead of OEWS employment counts, onet_pums_employment_weights() converts individual records to SOC-level weights. The SOCP and PWGTP names match common ACS PUMS output from tidycensus::get_pums().

pums <- tibble::tibble(
  SOCP = c("151252", "151252", "291141"),
  PWGTP = c(120, 80, 200)
)

onet_pums_employment_weights(pums)
#> # A tibble: 2 × 3
#>   soc_code employment records
#>   <chr>         <dbl>   <int>
#> 1 15-1252         200       2
#> 2 29-1141         200       1

For live ACS pulls, start with tidycensus and then pass the resulting data frame to onet_pums_employment_weights():

pums <- tidycensus::get_pums(
  variables = c("SOCP", "PWGTP", "WAGP"),
  state = "all",
  survey = "acs1",
  year = 2023
)

weights <- onet_pums_employment_weights(pums)

Real OEWS downloads

BLS publishes annual public downloads. These calls download and cache the corresponding file:

national <- onet_oews_national(2024)
states <- onet_oews_state(2024)
metros <- onet_oews_metro(2024)
industries <- onet_oews_industry(2024)

If automated downloads are rate-limited, download the ZIP manually from the BLS OEWS public data pages and pass the local path:

national <- onet_oews_national(2024, path = "oesm24nat.zip")

On this page
